data.title: Reaction Roundup: SA analysts, experts weigh in on soft September CPI data
data.description:
Wall Street's focus on Friday was on the long-awaited September consumer inflation report, which had been delayed due to the ongoing U.S. government shutdown. Market participants starved of economic data were rewarded with a cooler-than-anticipated consumer price index ((CPI)) reading.   

According to the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, headline CPI came in at +0.3% M/M, versus an expected figure of +0.4%. Core CPI for September was +0.2% M/M, versus a consensus of +0.3%. On a Y/Y basis, core CPI was +3.0%, still well above the Federal Reserve's 2% target.

The CPI print reinforced expectations that the Fed would deliver another 25 basis point interest rate cut at its monetary policy committee meeting next week. Meanwhile, the data could be the last inflation indicator for a while, as the White House said the October CPI report likely won't be released.

Wall Street jumped on the data, with all three major averages - the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500), the tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND), and the blue-chip Dow (DJI) - scaling new record highs. Here are some exchange-traded funds that track the S&P: (NYSEARCA:SPY), (NYSEARCA:VOO), (NYSEARCA:IVV), (NYSEARCA:RSP), (NYSEARCA:SSO), (NYSEARCA:UPRO), (NYSEARCA:SH), (NYSEARCA:SDS), and (NYSEARCA:SPXU).

See below for various reactions to the September CPI:

Mike Zaccardi, Seeking Alpha analyst since 2022: 

"The dated September CPI report was welcome news, but it doesn't change the Fed's likely course of action. It's also potentially the only official inflation look we'll have for the rest of the year, depending on the length of the government shutdown (now seen at 45 days, per the Kalshi betting market).

Investors are pleased, though, with the S&P 500 ETF notching a new high this morning. Bond yields and volatility fell, too. Eyes shift back to earnings next week, with Wednesday and Thursday being particularly busy. Also, keep in mind that the Fed's rate decision will be announced on Wednesday afternoon, ahead of the Trump/Xi meeting."

See Mike's full CPI reaction here. 

Leo Nelissen, part of investing group iREIT+HOYA Capital:

"Today was a great example of what we should get used to. Inflation may be getting stuck at 3%, 100bps above the Fed's target, but the market is happy it's not worse. I think 'smart money' knows we'll get lower rates to stimulate the economy, even if it means risking higher inflation."

Sarah House, senior economist at Wells Fargo:

"Today's softer-than-expected CPI data should lock the FOMC into a 25 bps rate cut at its meeting next week. That said, today's data were not so soft that the Committee can sound the all clear on inflation.

The underlying pace of consumer inflation appears stuck around 3%. With higher prices from tariffs still working their way into the economy, we expect inflation to remain sticky near this 3% level through the middle of next year."

Mohamed El-Erian, chief economic advisor at Allianz and former CEO of PIMCO:

"This report makes a Federal Reserve rate cut next week highly probable. What happens beyond that, however, will depend on subsequent data, primarily confirmation of a softening labor market and continued disinflation."

Jason Furman, former deputy director of the U.S. National Economic Council:

"I wish I knew what was happening on the employment and GDP side. But the inflation side is looking way above target. Financial conditions are easing. People are worried (about) a bubble. You would not normally cut interest rates at a time like this." 

Dario Perkins, managing director of global macro at GlobalData.TSLombard:

"Hard to know whether a soft CPI is good or bad when you have no other data to put it in context. If you could choose one piece of data on a desert island, you wouldn't choose the CPI. Yet that's where we are."

Ross Gerber, co-founder of Gerber Kawasaki Wealth & Investment Management:

"Inflation holding at 3%. With GDP running at 3%. Maybe not the economy the Fed is used to; however the 2% inflation goal usually works poorly for economic growth. Stocks like the numbers. Earnings are good too."

data.title: Clover Health jumps 19%; options activity could be why
data.description:
• Clover Health Investments (NASDAQ:CLOV) is up ~19% in Friday morning trading despite no new news releases, SEC filings, or other clear catalysts.
  • However, a check of options activity since today's market opening could provide some insights. Since then, 10 put options have been recorded.
  • The largest, with a volume of ~35,000 put options, has a strike price of $3.50. Another, with ~32,500 puts, is has a strike price of $4.00.
  • Only four call options have been recorded so far today, but all with relatively little volume.
  • Clover Health was also helped recently by an upgrade to buy from Zacks Investment Research. 
  • Speculation has also been brewing for months that a partnership between Clover Health's AI business, Counterpart Health, and Humana (HUM) could be coming.
  • Clover Health reports its Q3 financial results on Nov. 4 after market close.

data.title: Virtus Investment Q3 earnings fall short as net outflows stay high
data.description:
Virtus Investment Partners (NYSE:VRTS) shares slid 2.4% after the asset manager's third-quarter earnings came in worse than expected as net outflows persisted, although revenue beat

Q3 adjusted EPS of $6.69, missing the $6.77 average analyst estimate, rose from $6.25 in the prior quarter and fell from $6.92 in Q3 2024.

Revenue of $216.4M, surpassing the $197.2M expected, advanced from $191M in Q2 and retreated from $227.0M in the year-ago period.

Net outflows were $3.9B in Q3, unchanged from the prior quarter and up from the $1.7B of net outflows recorded a year before. Market performance and positive net flows in exchange-traded funds were more than offset by net outflows in other products during the quarter.

Total assets under management were $169.3B at Sept. 30, 2025, down slightly from $170.7B in Q2 and -8% Y/Y.

Total operating expenses, as adjusted, of $131.7M in Q3 vs. $131.2M in Q2 and $134.7M in Q3 2024.

data.title: Hilltop signals stable NII outlook with $0.74 EPS and leadership transition as competition intensifies
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Hilltop Holdings Inc. (HTH) Q3 2025

Management View

  • Jeremy Ford, President, CEO & Chairman, stated that "For the third quarter, Hilltop reported net income of approximately $46 million or $0.74 per diluted share. Return on average assets for the period was 1.2% and return on average equity was 8.35%." Ford highlighted continued expansion in net interest margin at PlainsCapital Bank, strong growth in core loan and deposit balances, and robust net revenue growth at HilltopSecurities across all four business lines.
  • Ford noted that PlainsCapital Bank produced strong core loan growth despite competitive Texas markets and saw core deposits increase by 6% on a linked-quarter basis. The quarter included a $2.6 million reversal of credit losses driven by improved asset quality. He also discussed PrimeLending's $7 million pretax loss due to subdued mortgage origination volumes, mentioning, "existing home sales across the country reached their lowest level in over 30 years."
  • HilltopSecurities delivered pretax income of $26.5 million on net revenues of $144.5 million, with public finance net revenues up 28% year-over-year and Wealth Management revenues increasing by $7 million compared to the third quarter of 2024.
  • Ford announced key management changes: "In November, PlainsCapital Bank's Chief Credit Officer, Darrell Adams, plans to retire," with Brent Raindl promoted to Chief Credit Officer and Thomas Ricks as the new Dallas Region Chairman.
  • William Furr, Executive VP & CFO, stated, "for the third quarter of 2025, Hilltop reported consolidated income attributable to common stockholders of $45.8 million, equating to $0.74 per diluted share." Furr highlighted net interest income of $112 million, a $2.5 million reversal of provision for credit losses, and a $1.3 million reduction in the allowance for unfunded reserves. He noted, "Hilltop's allowance for credit losses declined during the quarter by $2.8 million to $95 million, resulting in a coverage ratio of ACL to loans HFI of 1.16%."

Outlook

  • Furr provided that the internal rate outlook anticipates one additional 25 basis point rate cut in 2025, followed by two more in the first half of 2026. Under this scenario, "we expect that NII levels will remain relatively stable over the coming quarters with modest downward pressure during the seasonally weaker mortgage production period that typically occurs in the first quarter of 2026." He also expects interest-bearing deposit betas, which averaged approximately 70% through the current rate cut cycle, will gradually decline but remain above 60%.
  • The company expects full year average total loans to increase 0% to 2% from 2024 levels, excluding mortgage warehouse lending and any retained mortgages from PrimeLending.

Financial Results

  • Hilltop reported net interest income of $112 million for the third quarter, up $7.4 million or 7% from the prior year, driven by improving deposit costs and growth in higher-yielding commercial loans. Net interest margin increased by 5 basis points from the second quarter to 306 basis points.
  • Total noninterest income for the quarter was $218 million. Mortgage revenues declined by $3.4 million year-over-year as origination volumes stabilized but remained at lower levels. Gain on sale margins improved by 8 basis points to 226 basis points.
  • Noninterest expenses increased by $7.6 million from the prior year to $272 million, primarily due to higher variable compensation linked to broker-dealer revenue production.
  • Average HFI loans were $8.1 billion, with period ending loans growing by $166 million from Q2 2025, mainly due to new commercial real estate originations. Commercial lending pipelines expanded by over $750 million versus Q2.
  • NPA levels declined by $5.3 million from Q2 to $76.5 million. Net charge-offs for the quarter were $282,000.

Q&A

  • Michael Rose, Raymond James: Asked about the NII guide and margin trajectory. Furr explained, "We are and remain asset sensitive on the balance sheet...we do have an additional rate cut in the fourth quarter expected." He added that immediate step-down impacts from rate reductions affect NII, but new commercial loans are being booked at about 690 basis points.
  • Rose followed up on share repurchase strategy and M&A outlook. Ford responded, "we are trying to be more consistent with our share repurchases...why we've asked for the increase in authorization." On M&A, Ford noted the influx of out-of-market entrants into Texas and Hilltop's focus on opportunities arising from market dislocations.
  • Rose asked about auto portfolio exposures. Furr said, "we've been working our way through that portfolio" and have no direct exposure to a publicized entity in the sector.
  • Wood Lay, KBW: Inquired about subprime auto exposure. Furr acknowledged "certainly some subprime exposure there," but said the exposure is "appropriately reserved and appropriately being managed."
  • Lay asked about broker-dealer fee income and efficiency ratio. Furr highlighted strong public finance activity and acknowledged some episodic items contributed to the quarter's strength. He expects pretax margins to revert to low teens. Ford emphasized the positive performance in public finance and Wealth Management.
  • Jordan Ghent, Stephens: Asked about potential government shutdown impacts. Ford and Furr stated no primary effects for the broker-dealer, with only minor processing impacts in SBA and mortgage due to agency staffing.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts questioned sustainability of NII and fee income, and probed for risk in the auto loan portfolio and broker-dealer business, with a slightly cautious tone.
  • Management maintained a neutral-to-confident tone in prepared remarks, but responses in Q&A were more measured, especially around NII guidance and margin pressures, using phrases like "we are and remain asset sensitive" and "we feel like we've got it appropriately reserved."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's confidence in prepared remarks remained steady, but Q&A responses reflected heightened caution regarding margin compression and loan growth challenges. Analyst tone shifted from neutral to slightly more pressing, especially on recurring revenue and risk management topics.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Net income rose from $36 million in Q2 to $46 million in Q3, with EPS up from $0.57 to $0.74 per diluted share. Return on average equity improved to 8.35% from 6.6%.
  • Net interest income increased from $110.7 million to $112 million, while net interest margin expanded by 5 basis points compared to Q2. Noninterest income increased, driven by stronger HilltopSecurities results.
  • PrimeLending swung from a $3 million pretax gain in Q2 (benefiting from a nonrecurring legal settlement) to a $7 million pretax loss in Q3 amid continued mortgage market weakness.
  • HilltopSecurities' pretax margin improved significantly quarter-over-quarter, with all business lines performing strongly.
  • Average HFI loans and commercial lending pipelines both showed sequential growth, while NPA and net charge-off levels improved.
  • Management announced a leadership succession at PlainsCapital Bank, a development not seen in the previous quarter.
  • Both quarters reiterated a cautious outlook on loan growth and expense discipline, but Q3 guidance emphasized stability in NII and deposit betas amid anticipated further rate cuts.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing margin pressure from heightened competition in Texas and continued challenges in mortgage origination due to low home sales and affordability.
  • Furr noted, "there's been a flurry of recent credit news in the marketplace," but stated, "we do not anticipate any significant systemic risk across the portfolio at this time."
  • Subprime auto exposure was acknowledged but described as actively managed and reserved.
  • Broker-dealer fee income included some episodic items not expected to recur every quarter, and management expects a normalization of pretax margins.
  • Uncertainty around interest rates, inflation, and broader economic health continues to be a focus.

Final Takeaway

The third quarter saw Hilltop Holdings deliver higher net income and EPS, supported by strong results at PlainsCapital Bank and a standout performance at HilltopSecurities. While the company expects net interest income to remain stable in the near term, management is closely monitoring competitive pressures, credit quality, and the evolving rate environment. Strategic leadership transitions and an active capital return program underscore Hilltop's commitment to maintaining financial strength and pursuing growth opportunities amid persistent industry headwinds.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Trilogy Metals jumps as Ambler road permits reinstated
data.description:
Trilogy Metals (NYSE:TMQ) +13.3% in Friday's trading after saying the state of Alaska executed key right-of-way permits for its Ambler access project with the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers, the National Park Service and the Bureau of Land Management, re-establishing the federal authorizations required to advance the road project to the Ambler mining district.

The move follows this month's Presidential decision under Section 1106 of the Alaska National Interest Lands Conservation Act that directed agencies to reissue the authorizations originally granted in 2020.

The reinstated permits restore the 50-year right-of-way across federally managed lands, opening the way for the Alaska Industrial Development and Export Authority to resume road planning activities, including updates to engineering plans, budgets, and programs for next year.

data.title: Metropolitan Bank outlines 2026 margin expansion potential and accelerated deposit growth through new verticals
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Metropolitan Bank Holding Corp. (MCB) Q3 2025

Management View

  • Mark DeFazio, President and CEO, highlighted "strong balance sheet growth funded by core deposits" and a continued growth strategy underpinned by "unwavering commitment to risk management in all of its forms." Loan growth for the quarter was approximately $170 million or 2.6%, with year-to-date loan book growth exceeding $750 million or more than 12%. Total loan originations year-to-date were $1.4 billion, and core deposits grew by approximately $280 million or 4.1% in the quarter. Year-to-date deposit growth surpassed $1 billion or 18%, achieved without team acquisitions. DeFazio announced expansion into Lakewood, New Jersey, Miami, and West Palm Beach, Florida, with new branch openings. "The third quarter marked our eighth consecutive quarter of margin expansion. The net interest margin increased 5 basis points to 3.88%, up from 3.83% in the prior quarter." DeFazio also introduced a $50 million share repurchase program and noted the payment of the company’s first common stock dividend. He revealed ongoing implementation of a new technology stack, expected to be fully integrated by the end of Q1 2026, and the launch of an AI strategy following the hiring of the company's first AI Director.
  • DeFazio stated, "Our asset quality remains very strong with no broad-based negative trends identified in any loan segment, geography or sector impacting our portfolio." He emphasized the $23.9 million provision expense for the quarter, with $18.7 million related to three out-of-state multifamily loans extended to a single borrower group in 2021 and 2022, calling the specific reserve "a clear outlier."
  • Daniel Dougherty, Executive Vice President and CFO, commented, "MCB's strong performance in 2025 continued in the third quarter... our underwriting standards and loan pricing parameters have not all been altered to achieve our growth results and goals." He noted total originations and draws of approximately $583 million with a weighted average coupon net of fees of 7.27% for the quarter, and a mix of 70% fixed and 30% floating rate loans. Dougherty described the NBFI book as "about $350 million or about 5% of the loan portfolio" with all credits currently rated pass. He provided guidance that Q4 net interest margin (NIM) is expected to be between 3.90% and 3.95%, and annual NIM for 2025 "will be north of 3.80%." Dougherty added, "each 25 basis point cut in the Fed funds target rate will, all else being equal, drive about 5 basis points of NIM expansion annually."

Outlook

  • Management projects between $100 million and $200 million of additional loan growth for the remainder of the year, with a strong pipeline shaping up for the first quarter of 2026. Dougherty forecasted that Q4 NIM will be between 3.90% and 3.95% and annual NIM will be "north of 3.80%." Digital transformation project costs are expected to be approximately $3 million in Q4, and less than $2 million in Q1 2026. Detailed guidance for next year will be provided after Q4 earnings.

Financial Results

  • Net interest income for Q3 was $77.3 million, up 5% sequentially and more than 18% compared to the same quarter last year. Diluted EPS for the third quarter was reported at $0.67. CFO Dougherty estimated that adjusting for the Q3 provisioning, normalized diluted EPS would be approximately $1.95. Noninterest income stood at $2.5 million, essentially unchanged from the prior period. Noninterest expense was approximately $45.8 million, up $2.7 million from the previous quarter, with increases mainly in compensation, technology costs, and licensing. The FDIC assessment expense declined by $1 million, with a go-forward run rate of about $1.5 million per quarter. Q4 operating expenses are expected to be approximately $46 million, including $3 million in one-time digital project costs.

Q&A

  • Gregory Zingone, Piper Sandler: Asked for additional details on the out-of-state CRE multifamily relationship, including metrics and geography. DeFazio explained, "The geographies are Champagne, Illinois and a city in Ohio. These are basically vacant buildings that were going to be renovated and then stabilized... we're at a point now where we are working through a restructuring with the client and cautiously optimistic that a material part of this specific reserve will be reversed in either the fourth quarter or the first quarter of next year."
  • Zingone: Inquired about the $5.2 million CECL provisioning. Dougherty said, "We rely on a third-party vendor to provide the reasonable and supportable forecast for macroeconomic variables, Moody's who we use... our model is highly levered to that index. And so it's not aligned generally with our specific concerns, but those macroeconomic variables as forecasted by Moody's drive the result."
  • Zingone: Asked about insider selling prior to earnings releases. DeFazio responded that all insider trading agreements are under 10b5-1 plans and there is no violation of blackout periods.
  • Feddie Strickland, Hovde Group: Sought details about out-of-market CRE loan share. Dougherty confirmed, "no other immediate concerns about other CRE, whether in market or out of market at this juncture."
  • Strickland: Asked about deposit growth opportunities. DeFazio emphasized, "We have so many different deposit verticals. We don't have to rely on any one of them to drive 10%, 15% or even 20% balance sheet growth."
  • Strickland: Inquired about digital transformation costs. Dougherty projected a tail of less than $2 million in digital expenses for Q1 2026.
  • David Konrad, KBW: Asked about the size of the credit issue. DeFazio confirmed, "One was around $8 million, one was around $17 million. And I believe the third one -- the total was around $34 million."
  • Konrad: Asked about the impact on growth strategies. DeFazio replied, "No. And this is an outlier that we'll work through it, but we just felt it was prudent to take this specific reserve. Remember, it's not a charge-off."
  • Strickland (follow-up): Asked whether margin could approach 4% in 2026 with multiple rate cuts. Dougherty responded, "Very much so Feddie. Very much so. Yes. We continue to be liability sensitive slightly, modestly. My forecasting, yes, we here is 4% when I look at that."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts questioned management on provisioning, asset quality, out-of-market exposures, digital transformation costs, and the sustainability of deposit growth, indicating a slightly cautious but inquisitive tone throughout the session.
  • Management maintained a confident and measured tone in both prepared remarks and responses, emphasizing prudent provisioning, asset quality, and robust growth outlook. Confidence was underscored by statements such as "we are confident that these new technologies will support and scale with MCB's diversified and growing commercial bank for years to come" and "we remain committed to managing asset quality and optimizing profitability."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst tone remained focused on credit and deposit growth sustainability, while management continued to project steady confidence, particularly regarding asset quality and growth initiatives.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Loan and deposit growth moderated slightly in Q3 compared to Q2, but remained strong, with year-to-date figures surpassing $750 million for loans and $1 billion for deposits. Margin expansion persisted, with NIM rising to 3.88% from 3.83% in Q2. Strategic focus shifted to technology integration and AI, while Q2 emphasized share repurchases and the inaugural dividend. Provision expense rose sharply in Q3 due to a significant reserve for a single borrower, compared to lower provisioning in Q2. Analysts in both quarters concentrated on credit quality, deposit verticals, and digital transformation, with management maintaining a consistent tone of disciplined optimism.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited no broad-based negative trends in any loan segment, geography, or sector. The $23.9 million provision expense, with $18.7 million for three multifamily loans, was described as a specific outlier. Macro-driven CECL provisioning was flagged, tied to "forecast deterioration" in the CRE price index per Moody's model. Analyst concerns centered on the possibility of additional out-of-market CRE issues, digital transformation costs, and the sustainability of growth. Management reiterated commitment to robust risk management and ongoing customer engagement to identify market stress.

Final Takeaway

Metropolitan Bank Holding Corp. highlighted consistent balance sheet and margin growth, underpinned by disciplined risk management and diversified funding. Management affirmed readiness to capitalize on favorable banking industry tailwinds, with robust loan and deposit pipelines, ongoing technology transformation, and prudent capital actions including share repurchases and dividends. Asset quality remains strong, and despite a notable provision for a specific credit, management expects continued growth and margin expansion into 2026, driven by core deposits, strategic new verticals, and operational enhancements.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Stellar Bancorp signals focus on disciplined growth and margin defense following $25.7M Q3 net income
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Stellar Bancorp, Inc. (STEL) Q3 2025

Management View

  • CEO Robert Franklin opened the call by stating, "I'm pleased to report that we delivered solid results, including increasing our net interest income and our net interest margin. Our balance sheet expansion was driven primarily by deposit growth, reflecting our bankers' emphasis on getting the full client relationship." Franklin addressed credit quality, highlighting, "While we experienced some charge-offs in the quarter, they were spread over several small credits, most of which were already identified and appropriately reserved. We feel comfortable at our present level of reserve based on our portfolio and the markets that we serve." He further emphasized, "As we continue to strengthen our capital position, we have repurchased shares, and we have paid down $30 million of our subordinated debt just after quarter end. Our well-capitalized position gives us valuable flexibility and we remain committed to deploying capital in ways to enhance our shareholder value."
  • CFO Paul Egge reported, "We are pleased to report third quarter 2025 net income of $25.7 million or $0.50 per diluted share as compared to net income of $26.4 million or $0.51 per share in the second quarter." Egge highlighted improvements in net interest income and margin, stating, "Net interest income was $100.6 million, representing an increase from the $98.3 million booked in the second quarter, largely due to higher earning assets and net interest margin for the quarter. This translated into the net interest margin of 4.2% relative to 4.18% posted in the second quarter." He noted, "Our expense increased to $73.1 million from $70 million in the second quarter, primarily due to an increase in salaries and benefits into a lesser extent, increases in professional fees and advertising." Egge described the quarter's expenses as "an outlier" and expects fourth quarter expenses to be closer to the run rate for the first half of the year.

Outlook

  • Management expressed a disciplined approach to growth, with Franklin stating, "We believe that if we continue to be disciplined in building quality assets protecting margins and focusing on full balance relationships, we will drive long-term value for our shareholders."
  • Egge shared, "We continue to feel good about our ability to defend and perhaps incrementally improve on our top-tier margin profile by focusing on staying true to our core relationship banking model."
  • The company anticipates fourth quarter expenses to return closer to the run rate from the first half of the year, in contrast to the higher third quarter expenses.

Financial Results

  • Net income for the third quarter was $25.7 million or $0.50 per diluted share. Net interest income was $100.6 million, and net interest margin was 4.2%. Excluding purchase accounting accretion, net interest income increased to $95.9 million, with net interest margin (excluding accretion) reaching 4%.
  • The company booked a provision for loan losses of $305,000, with $3.3 million in net charge-offs relating to over 10 relationships, "most of these were previously identified and already specifically reserved for." Allowance for credit losses on loans ended at $78.9 million or 1.1% of loans.
  • Noninterest income was $5 million, a decrease from $5.8 million in the prior quarter, largely due to $445,000 of write-downs on foreclosed assets and lower other noninterest income.
  • Noninterest expense rose to $73.1 million, driven by higher salaries and benefits, severance expenses related to upcoming branch closures ($0.5 million), and elevated medical insurance costs.
  • Total risk-based capital was 16.33% at quarter end. Year-over-year, tangible book value per share increased 9.3% to $21.08 per share after dividends and share repurchases totaling just under $5 million for the quarter.

Q&A

  • David Feaster, Raymond James: Asked about drivers behind payoffs and pay downs, competitive dynamics, and growth outlook. President Ramon Vitulli responded that payoffs were about $50 million higher than the previous quarter, with 44% related to sale of collateral or business and 25% to competitive refinancing. Vitulli stated, "We're really pleased with the originations last -- third quarter, we originated almost $500 million of loans compared to $640 million the previous quarter."
  • Feaster questioned credit quality and approach. CFO Egge described stress testing and portfolio monitoring, expressing comfort due to the dynamic markets served and a shift to stickier C&I credits.
  • Feaster asked about deposit growth. Vitulli reported 51% of new deposits were from new customers and credited brand awareness and customer satisfaction, while Franklin noted, "We just feel very strongly that low-cost deposits is something that everyone is going to be fighting over, and it's something we put a big emphasis on."
  • Stephen Scouten, Piper Sandler: Inquired about seasonal deposit strength and expenses. Egge noted seasonal government banking deposits impact the fourth quarter, not the current period. On expenses, Egge clarified, "We will see some inflation ... but the goal is to continue optimizing and holding the line as much as we can going into 2026 and beyond."
  • William Jones, KBW: Asked about net interest margin defense in a Fed easing cycle. Egge replied, "We're going to be focused on lowering deposit costs, where we can," and expressed confidence in defending the 4% NIM excluding purchase accounting accretion.
  • Matt Olney, Stephens: Asked about timing of payoffs slowing and M&A prospects. Vitulli anticipated a lift as advances exceed paydowns in coming quarters, while Franklin reiterated a cautious approach to M&A to protect the deposit base.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' questions reflected a neutral to slightly positive tone, focusing on growth, credit quality, deposit strength, expenses, and margin defense, with no significant skepticism or pressing tone detected.
  • Management maintained a confident and measured tone, emphasizing discipline, capital strength, and margin defense. Egge's remarks on expenses as an "outlier" and Franklin's focus on relationship banking reinforced a steady and optimistic narrative.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, sentiment remained consistent, with ongoing confidence in the company's strategy and financial footing.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language remained disciplined, with management reiterating long-term value creation through quality asset growth and margin defense.
  • Strategic focus shifted slightly toward capital deployment flexibility following the paydown of subordinated debt and lighter share repurchases relative to earlier quarters.
  • Key metrics: Net interest income and margin improved quarter-over-quarter, while noninterest income declined. Expenses increased due to one-time items and higher employee costs.
  • Analysts' focus persisted on growth, credit, deposits, and expenses, with management's confidence in margin defense and relationship-driven growth consistent with prior calls.
  • M&A interest continues, but with an emphasis on protecting the balance sheet and funding base.

Risks and Concerns

  • Credit quality remains a headline issue, with charge-offs spread across small credits already identified and reserved. Management stated comfort with current reserve levels and low exposure to nonoriginated credits.
  • Elevated payoffs and paydowns, especially from competitive refinancing and collateral sales, are noted as ongoing challenges.
  • Expense pressures arose from severance and medical insurance; management described these as temporary and expects normalization.
  • The competitive market for low-cost deposits and the risk of "credit light" covenant packages were highlighted as industry-wide challenges. Management stressed ongoing discipline and selectivity.

Final Takeaway

Stellar Bancorp's third quarter results highlight a disciplined growth strategy, with improvements in net interest income and margin supported by healthy deposit inflows and a robust capital position. Management remains focused on maintaining asset quality, defending margins, and optimizing expenses, even as competitive dynamics and payoff pressures persist in the Texas market. Efforts to grow the loan book, attract new customers, and manage costs are central to the company’s outlook, while M&A remains a possibility only if it aligns with preserving the company’s funding and balance sheet strength.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Papa John's gains as BofA moves to no rating amid takeover reports
data.description:
Papa John's (NASDAQ:PZZA) rose 2.3% as a BofA analyst moved to no rating from neutral amid recent takeover reports. 

The pizza chain is no longer trading on fundamentals after recent takeover reports, BofA analyst Sara Senatore wrote in a note on Friday. She cited a Reuters report from last Wednesday that Apollo Global (APO) submitted a takeover bid for $64 a share.

"While details are limited and the parties have not confirmed the reports and no deal is guaranteed, we believe the market is discounting a high likelihood that PZZA will be acquired," Senatore wrote.

Reuters reported in June that Apollo and Irth Capital Management submitted a joint offer for Papa John's (NASDAQ:PZZA) at just above $60 per share.

Papa John's (PZZA) is set to report Q3 results on Nov. 6.

data.title: Molina cut to Underweight at Barclays despite earnings-led selloff
data.description:
Molina Healthcare (NYSE:MOH) bounced back to add ~2% in early trading on Friday, even as Barclays downgraded the health insurer, despite the stock’s ~17% drop in the previous session following its Q3 miss.

Analyst Andrew Mok downgraded the Long Beach, California-based managed care firm to Underweight from Neutral and lowered its price target to $144 from $185 per share, citing risks to the company’s forward earnings per share.

During the earnings call, Molina (NYSE:MOH) CFO Mark Keim indicated that the company’s 2026 EPS would likely approach its 2025 guidance, which the company revised down on Wednesday to $14 from the prior forecast of at least $19, citing adverse cost trends in its business segments.

However, according to Mok, the firm has set its preliminary outlook for 2026, assuming an overly optimistic flat medical loss ratio for its Medicaid business next year.

“Simply put, we think the company's preliminary view of 2026 EPS of $14 is too optimistic and underpinned by the assumption that Medicaid MLR is flat y/y,” he wrote, projecting an $11 per share forecast for MOH’s 2026 EPS, 21% below the initial view.

data.title: Snap reportedly looking to raise $1B for Specs unit; Stifel cuts rating to 'sell'
data.description:
Snapchat parent Snap Inc. (NYSE:SNAP) is in discussions with potential investors, including Saudi Arabia's Public Investment Fund, to raise at least a billion dollars to bolster the company's AR glasses operations, according to a report by tech news website Sources.

The report, citing people familiar with the matter, said CEO Evan Spiegel has been holding talks with various parties to raise capital over the past couple of months. Spiegel is scheduled to speak onstage at the fund’s Future Investment Initiative in Riyadh next week.

The fundraising details haven’t been finalized, and talks with Saudi's PIF are ongoing, the report said.

Snap plans to turn its Specs hardware unit into an independent subsidiary—similar to Waymo, which operates independently within Google's parent company, Alphabet—while continuing to raise money from investors, the report said, citing sources.

In September, Saudi investor Prince Al Waleed bin Talal Al Saud boosted his stake in Snap by 0.5% to 2.8% by purchasing 15.2M shares. The prince currently holds 40.8M shares in the Santa Monica, California-based company, which, as per the last closing price of $7.87, is valued at over $321M.

Separately, Snap's investment rating was downgraded by research firm Stifel to "sell" from "hold" on concerns the company will continue to lose market share, which will hurt ad revenue growth next year.

"Our checks continue to skew negative, and with incremental confidence TikTok is not going away in the U.S., we believe this trend is unlikely to reverse any time soon," Stifel analyst Mark Kelley said. The price target was lowered to $6.50 from $8, implying a downside of more than 17%.
